metadata.career_relevance: Zerodha's Kite app shows live stock prices updating every second. Without subscriptions: browser polls every second (1000 users × 1 req/s = 1000 req/s just for price updates). With GraphQL subscriptions: 1000 persistent WebSocket connections, server pushes only when price changes. Zerodha processes 10 million+ trade updates per day. After this topic, you can build real-time dashboards that scale.

hook.real_world_problem:
Zerodha Kite without subscriptions:

```
1000 users watching RELIANCE live price
Each browser polls every second:
  1000 users × 1 request/s = 1000 HTTP requests/s
  Each request: Spring handles, DB reads, response sent
  Server: 1000 connections opened/closed per second
  Latency: up to 1s behind real price (poll interval)

With GraphQL subscriptions (WebSocket):
  1000 persistent WebSocket connections (kept open)
  Server pushes ONLY when price changes
  Average: 30 price changes/minute per stock
  Traffic: 1000 users × 30/min = 500 pushes/min (vs 60,000 polls/min)
  Server load: 120× less
  Latency: <50ms from trade execution to screen update
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Zerodha live price subscription and Razorpay payment status subscription.

concept_explanation.schema:
**Subscription in GraphQL schema:**

```graphql
type Subscription {
  # Subscribe to live price updates for a stock symbol:
  priceUpdate(symbol: String!): PriceUpdate!

  # Subscribe to payment status changes:
  paymentStatus(paymentId: String!): PaymentStatus!

  # Subscribe to ALL trade updates (admin use):
  tradeUpdates: Trade!
}

type PriceUpdate {
  symbol: String!
  price: Float!
  change: Float!
  timestamp: String!
}
```

visual_aid.description: Two-part diagram. Left: HTTP polling (bad) — Browser → Server: repeated arrows labeled 'GET /prices every 1s', each arrow has a response arrow back. 1000 users = 1000 req/s. Right: WebSocket subscription (good) — 1000 browser arrows connect once to Server (WebSocket upgrade). Server has a single Flux<PriceUpdate> publishing to all connected clients via subscription protocol. Only one arrow per price change (not one per user). Price update source: Trade Engine → Publisher → Flux → all subscribed WebSockets. Labels: 'graphql-ws: subprotocol over WebSocket', 'Flux<T>: reactive stream from server'.
visual_aid.alt_text: GraphQL subscriptions: WebSocket push vs HTTP polling comparison

worked_example.scenario: Zerodha live RELIANCE price subscription with Flux.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. PriceUpdate record + subscription schema
2. @SubscriptionMapping priceUpdate(symbol): returns Flux<PriceUpdate>
3. Flux.interval(1s) → simulate price updates
4. @SpringBootTest: Flux emits correct type, completes after N elements

step_by_step_solution.details: HTTP: stateless, each request opens a new connection. Long-polling: keep connection open until data available — wastes threads. Server-Sent Events (SSE): one-way, server → client, HTTP. WebSocket: bidirectional, persistent, efficient for many clients. GraphQL subscriptions: use WebSocket with graphql-ws protocol. Spring for GraphQL: automatically handles the WebSocket upgrade and graphql-ws handshake when you add the WebSocket starter and configure spring.graphql.websocket.path.

things_to_notice: Flux<T> is the return type: Spring for GraphQL automatically bridges Reactor Flux to the WebSocket subscription protocol. Each element emitted by the Flux becomes one subscription message to the client.
@Argument: works the same as in @QueryMapping. The client passes the symbol when subscribing; Spring injects it into the method parameter.
takeUntil: for payment status, stop the subscription once a terminal state is reached (CAPTURED, FAILED). Without this, the subscription stays open indefinitely even after payment is complete.

Backpressure: if a client is slow (network lag), the Flux backpressure strategy determines what happens. onBackpressureDrop() drops overflow messages (fine for price data — client will see the next update). For payment status: use onBackpressureBuffer() (can't miss a status change).

industry_examples.use_case: RELIANCE price subscription: Flux.create() receives messages from an internal Kafka topic (NSE price feed). Each price tick: Kafka consumer → Sinks.Many → subscribed Flux → all WebSocket clients watching RELIANCE. 10 million price updates/day across 5000 symbols. GraphQL subscription filtering: only send price to clients subscribed to that specific symbol.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How would you build a real-time order tracking system like Swiggy?' → GraphQL subscriptions. Schema: subscription { orderStatus(orderId: String): OrderUpdate }. Resolver: Flux that filters order status events by orderId. Spring: @SubscriptionMapping returns Flux. WebSocket: spring.graphql.websocket.path. Client subscribes once, server pushes each status change. Terminal state: takeUntil(status -> status.isDelivered()) stops the subscription. Compare: polling = 60 req/min × 1000 users = 60,000 req/min. Subscriptions = 1000 WebSocket connections + 1 push per status change.
applications.real_world_workflow: Subscription workflow: (1) schema: add Subscription type with fields, (2) resolver: @SubscriptionMapping returns Flux<T>, (3) event source: Sinks.Many for push-based, Flux.interval for periodic, (4) filtering: .filter() to route events to correct subscriber, (5) termination: .takeUntil() for finite subscriptions, (6) configure: spring.graphql.websocket.path in application.yml, (7) test: StepVerifier or GraphQlTester.Subscriptions.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using blocking calls inside a Flux: `Flux.interval().map(t -> blockingRepository.findPrice(symbol))`. This blocks the scheduler thread for every subscription event — kills concurrency under load.
common_gaps.remediation: Use reactive repositories (R2DBC, reactive Mongo) inside Flux, or offload to a boundedElastic scheduler: `.publishOn(Schedulers.boundedElastic()).map(t -> blockingRepo.find(symbol))`. For price feeds: use Sinks.Many (non-blocking push from Kafka consumer) rather than polling the DB.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: JPA/JDBC calls directly inside Flux.map() — blocking call in reactive context warning from Reactor's debug agent

key_takeaways: GraphQL subscriptions: real-time server push over WebSocket
@SubscriptionMapping: returns Flux<T> (one emission = one message to client)
graphql-ws protocol: Spring for GraphQL handles automatically
.filter(event -> event.id().equals(clientId)): route events to correct subscriber
.onBackpressureDrop(): lose slow messages (OK for prices, NOT for payments)
WebSocket vs polling: 120× less server load for live data

self_check_questions.question: Razorpay processes a payment. The client subscribed to paymentStatus(paymentId: 'PAY-001'). How should the server know to push the update to this specific client?
self_check_questions.answer: Event filtering via Flux.filter(). The architecture: (1) Payment service publishes status changes to a Sinks.Many (in-memory bus) or Kafka topic. (2) The @SubscriptionMapping method subscribes to this event source and adds a .filter(event -> event.paymentId().equals(paymentId)). (3) Only events matching the client's paymentId are emitted through this Flux. (4) .takeUntil(event -> event.isFinal()) stops the Flux once CAPTURED or FAILED is received. Scale: 10,000 clients each watching a different payment — all share the same Sinks.Many source, each filters their own paymentId. The Flux.filter() is the subscriber-specific routing logic.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Final Module 27 topic: Testing GraphQL APIs — GraphQlTester, @GraphQlTest, testing mutations and subscriptions, error assertions.
